sentences.text: Not a whole-- just a tiny little--
--bit of the second board.
Yeah.
Then some youngsters did push-- not themselves.
They got to pushed from the inside to the outside by the people who have still been inside, the young-- the young one who had-- who wanted to get out.
And most of them just jumped.
But by that time, it was already night, dark and rainy.
And on the last carriage of the train, on the roof sat a Ukrainian with an automatic-- what do you call this gun which goes--?
Machine gun.
And he-- as soon as he heard some movement, he-- of course, he shot.
Some made it.
Others didn't.
And what happened to you?
I got also pushed out by the people from inside to the outside.
But I didn't jump.
I hung on by my hands and went around-- went around the corner and let myself down in between the rails and laid down, let the train pass over me.
And since it was dark and rainy and the Ukrainian has been shooting on the sides, never thinking that there could be somebody under the train.
After the train went away, I got up.
And where were you?
I've been one station-- was it one or two stations?-- before Otwock, the line which I knew very well.
I got up, went to the station, walked around.
I have seen a station master.
And he looked good.
He looked like a decent Pole.
And he was also from the resistance.
So he's taking me to his little hut.
I washed myself properly, because you can imagine laying under the train, and cleaned myself up and went back by train to Warsaw.
To the flat.
And at the window of the flat, Vera has been sitting for the last three or four nights, waiting for me to come back.
She left shortly before the last aktion on my path.
In the second aktion, I've stayed overnight on the Polish side and stupidly didn't ring to get to know if I can come in, if it's OK to come back into the ghetto, and just walked in.
--came to the gate--
No.
There haven't been very many phones in the ghetto.
And the few people who had the phones it wasn't worth-- there have been a few phones.
Real rich people had it and the one whom the Germans wanted to contact at any time and this sort of thing.
And the Judenrat, they had phones.
Well, I came to the gate.
And I didn't know that inside the ghetto, the aktion is there, that people are being rounded up and sent away.
And the SS on the gate asked me, how do you come now?
Where have you been all night?
And I lied a little from the sky, where I have been and what have been doing, all those sort of things.
Ah, yes, that I had to deliver some papers and-- to the Germans.
And it was too late to come back because the gates have been already closed.
And he didn't believe me.
And he said, oh, well, I will shoot you anyway just in case.
And he tried to shoot, but his pistol has jammed twice.
So he said, [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH].
You know what that means?
A devil sits in you.
So instead of shooting, he grabbed his pistol by the-- by the wrong side.
Yeah, and hit me across the face with it.
[LAUGHS] And I had a good blue mark.
And I came home, which wasn't very far from the gate.
And I have been asked by Vera, what happened to your face?
I have been ashamed to say that I have been so stupid not to ring.
Oh, I had a little accident.
I never admitted that I got hit.
Which-- what?
I managed to put it out of my mind.
Well, there are some things which come back.
But you know, I'm not a person who will give in.
So I say to myself, it's over, finished.
Well, we missed quite a few things.
For example-- oh, that was really later.
After the ghetto was liquidated and we lived on the Polish side in [NON-ENGLISH] Street, Grandma kept the house for us.
Vera was afraid to put her nose out, because she looked more Jewish than I did.
And she also didn't know how, but she acquired some Jewish accent.
And that was also a very giveaway thing.
And the kids, Roman and Eva, they got to this schools, but not real schools.
They have been the trade schools.
[BACKGROUND NOISE] Excuse me.
They both went to trade schools, and they both did work.
He worked in a metal work shop.
While in ghetto, he attended the Judenrat, the [? council ?] school, too, this in the same-- in the same line, metal work.
And Eva, she went to work in a little factory making paper bags.
[LAUGHS] They both worked.
And I went every day in the morning out, because I had a hundred little things to do and little shopping.
Eva.
Of course.
And Mina-- that was Vera's daughter-- she was so blonde and blue-eyed that nobody would ever suspect even that she wouldn't be Jewish.
Yes.
No, because we always lived in the Polish way.
We never lived in the-- for example, we never spoke Yiddish.
I don't know-- I didn't know a word of Yiddish till I came to the DP camp after the war.
And that was something which wouldn't give us away.
And my brother, Roman, he was not circumcised.
That was also something.
Because my parents didn't believe in marking the child.
They-- they've been 10 when the war broke out.
And he-- 10 and a bit.
He was 15 or 14-- no, 14 and a half, not quite 15-- when we had bandits.
You know, during the war there had been robberies [INAUDIBLE] just like there are now.
And we had also such robberies-- robberies and bandits coming.
And of course, they also suspected that they were Jewish.
Well, the first thing they did to Roman-- put your pants down.
But wasn't.
They didn't know, whether you were to think if it is or it isn't.
They grabbed a few things, like the [? portraits ?] and things which had been lying around, and made off.
But-- yeah?
Oh, yes.
Oh, my word.
Kids are 14.
They understand.
Mina didn't.
She was called Yasha-- [?
Yamina. ?] So it was Yasha.
The same as I did.
Roman [?
Bockatski, ?] Eva [?
Bockatska. ?]
Yes, that's right.
And Vera's husband-- Mina didn't know that her father was shot and that he was dead.
And we did tell, of course, all the neighbors and people we came in contact with that her husband has got work in-- as a part of Poland and he will only come now in the day.
Oh, something came from selling off things which we still had, like valuable pictures or jewelry or-- and then I worked a bit, too.
Yes.
My word. ?]
For some time, I worked in [NON-ENGLISH].
[NON-ENGLISH] was Warsaw city cleaning department.
They've been sending out cleaning trucks with water in the streets and this sort of thing.
Because my friends from the resistance-- they were there.
So I got in there, too.
But I got in only part time, not permanently.
But it was enough to put on a front-- to put up a front that you have--
Yes.
Yes.
She got one.
A false one.
Yeah.
Well, it wasn't terribly good, but-- I remember going every two, three days into the market, and horse flesh-- horse meat, which is lovely, you know?
Horse meat, when you make it properly, it's beautiful.
I really got to like it.
And vegetable.
Yes, because the radios-- they all had-- the resistance people, they had the radios.
We did know.
Not only did we know, but we traced on the map how the Russian front is coming closer, where they are now and--
Do you--?
Yes.
Yes, I know.
I have been in contact with my friends from the council, the Lichtenbaums.
They'd been transported to the Lublin Ghetto.
And they've been not straightaway put into the gas chambers, into Majdanek.
But they've been kept in the arbeitslager, in the work camp.
And I kept in contact with them through a driver from the Judenrat, from the council's car, a driver.
When the council-- when the ghettos stopped existing, he was still a friend.
And through him, I kept in contact.
He drove to Lublin and got through his friends some message to the people.
And I also got for the Lichtenbaums a flat.
They had a housemate who was very loyal to them.
I got the flat, and she kept it for them.
But they didn't make it.
They did not make it.
And they didn't make it because as Maitec wrote to me, we can't come because we have two enormous, big, old packets, the two mothers.
They didn't want to leave the mothers.
That happened to more than to one family.
They didn't want to separate.
They wanted to leave the [INAUDIBLE] or the mothers, and they all perished.
I've seen a woman from Kalisz, a Mrs.
Praszcha, who pushed her boy, a little boy, maybe eight years.
And he got taken away.
And she remained.
Some people did this.
Others wouldn't.
When there was aktion.
She did let the child to be taken, but not herself.
Because she wanted to stay.
She offered the child.
She later was taken away, too.
If it affected more the women than the men?
In a different way?
Well, that all depends what kind of people-- the thinking people, and that could have been women as well as men--
The thinking people, the people who--
Yes, intellectual, who could see what's happening, have been affected in different ways.
Some believed that they can make it, that they can get out of the whole situation.
Others have been absolutely determined that they were not.
I remember one-- the father of one of my friends when were marched into the wagons.
He jumped out from the queue and faced the German with a rifle, who had been minding the  queue going into the wagons.
And he said to him, shoot me now.
And the German said, no, you're going to-- into the train.
There have been people reacting differently.
No, I don't agree with this.
No.
The women had been working.
They had to work.
At the beginning-- at the beginning yes.
Well--
21 when--
No time for romance.
[LAUGHTER]
Yes, there have been marriages.
There have been romances.
There have been marriages.
Of course there have been.
But not everybody has been so busy.
[LAUGHS]
And with other things on your mind.

Yes, what I wanted to tell you is that after a couple of years, Roman-- that was the boy-- he has decided after the bandits and after they have checked him out and all the rest of it, he has decided that he would like to get on by himself.
Because he being not circumcised, he's fairly safe.
But with all the other women and children, he may not be as well off.
And Vera and I did not put our foot down.
If you think that you will be safer by yourself, OK.
We let him go.
And he volunteered for work in Germany.
Into a camp where a German factory, who has been taking on laborers from-- because the Germans have been in the army.
So they needed laborers from other nations.
And he went into such a camp.
Kassel.
Pardon?
In Westphalia.
Well, we've talked, Vera and I.
We talked about it quite some.
We came to the conclusion that he's nearly 15, and if he really feels safer and better, how can we say no?
If anything happens later, then it will be our responsibility.
And he was old enough to look after himself.
And he wanted it.
How can you force somebody who feel safer by himself?
Well, sometimes I worked in the cleaning department.
And sometimes I went shopping.
Sometimes I just went out to be out, to make the impression that you go, that you don't always stick around.
Wait a minute.
After those bandits came, we arranged for another-- for another safe house, where my friends from the resistance lived.
And Vera, Mina, and Eva, the three, they went over there, got new Kennkarten.
And grandma and I, we stayed.
No, they changed.
They changed, And grandma and I, we stayed in the flat to see what happens.
Oh, the mail just came in.
Never mind.

And a few days later-- two days later, not just a few-- just two days later, the same bandits-- I've been coming back from the market, and they found me and put a gun to my back and led me back to the flat.
But I had not been going there.
Just before the flat-- the entrance to the flat on the right-hand side-- there was a German [NON-ENGLISH].
I've been walking in front with a gun to the back.
And when I came to the German [NON-ENGLISH], I didn't go forward, but I jumped to the side.
[LAUGHS]
And the man did fire the gun.
The Germans from the [NON-ENGLISH] came out to see what's happening.
And I told them, there was a man who went shooting me.
And why would they want to shoot you?
And this.
And so-- and finally, they've taken my grandmother and myself into the Aleja Szucha.
That was the Gestapo headquarters.
And they said, you reported the bandits came there.
All right.
With my grandmother, who only spoke German and couldn't speak well Polish and with myself, we reported our incident, not saying that we've been suspected of being Jewish or anything like that.
At that time-- So the bandits-- sorry-- were Poles, were they?
Yes.
At that time, it was very common in Warsaw, the robberies, like-- like it is here today, or even more.
My friends from the resistance, which have been still friends from Kalisz, from the time when we lived in Kalisz.
Wait a minute.
Another queer coincidence-- as I told you before, my uncle was a president of the town.
And has provided jobs for the caretaker's children in-- one was a message boy, and the other one was something else in the magistrate, as it was called, town hall.
And we became real friends with his kids.
And during the war, they came to Warsaw.
And then there was another little-- another girl, who was a year behind me at school and whom I have been helping with her school work.
And we also became friends.
And they came to Warsaw.
And they joined the Polish resistance.
And they have been my friends.
These people have taken care of all sort of things.
And a coincidence-- this family, some of them have been left in Kalisz.
But some, the older one, came into Warsaw.
And one girl got taken by force to a-- a work camp in Germany to workers.
And it was a camp to which Roman came to.
And she recognized him, of course.
And she said to me much later, after the war, I have seen correspondence coming to him.
Roman was his name.
And Eva was on the envelope, who has been writing it.
Now, I had no more doubts it's the same Roman.
And she helped him all through the time when they've been in the same workers' camp.
But a coincidence of out-- out of all the workers' camps and out of all the peoples of different nationalities sent to the camps, these two kids came together again.
He corresponds with her even now.
When I needed a certificate, she gave it to me, only recently.
During the uprising, I've been in Warsaw itself.
There was another Jewish man, also in the army.
He was in the proper army, though he came across to me with his rifle on his back and gave me all the information, because I have not been in the shooting, so to say, army.
I have been in the one which have just been gathering information.
Anyway, the arising has died out, because Polish and Russian Army on the other side of the Vistula wouldn't come in while there was still a Warsaw.
They waited till the Germans have finished off so that the Polish communist government could take over.
And the people who survived have been marched out of the city into one of the stations-- railway stations past Warsaw.
Because the railway station of Warsaw itself, the three railway stations, have been flattened.
Well, and while we've been marched out, I skipped away.
Well, it was towards evening, and there was a highway.
And on both sides of the highways have been ditches, fairly deep ditches.
So I hopped into one of those ditches.
Out of the ditch and into a building, which turned out to be a bakery.

And this bakery has been supplying bread and rolls and all sort of things to the local villages-- not local, the surrounding villages, and, among other villages, the one where my sister and grandma and Eva and Mina have been.
So I got with a bread cart and went back to my people.
No, no, no-- well, way before the uprising.
You see, when we lost the flat where the bandits came, we went for some time into a safe house where my friends, the resistance people, lived.
But I felt it wasn't safe there.
[BACKGROUND NOISE] Though we provide-- they provided for me in a village, real village, not any more little town or anything, a summer house.
Because well-to-do people have been still going for the summer months out of the city.
So we went from their place into this place in [INAUDIBLE].
And one day I came back, because I had to come back to be in contact with the resistance.
And they've been taken away from two places.
Three people, [INAUDIBLE], [INAUDIBLE], and Vodak, have been from one place, where we stayed just before going to [INAUDIBLE].
And from another address in Warsaw, another two men-- they all were taken away by the Gestapo on the one day.
And one survived.
And I've met him in Kalisz after the war.
All the others have never been seen again.
And I asked him, when you've been arrested, when you've been taken away, what did they accuse you of, of hiding Jews?
And he said, no, there has never been any question.
We've been taken to the Gestapo and a bit questioned, tortured, but not one question was about us helping or hiding any Jews.
It was all only about the resistance.
And we never gave any other names.
We've been real friends.
And this boy-- young man, I would say-- he got finished in Bergen-Belsen and was freed by the Russians, I think.
Or was it American?
